I don’t have access to past conversation history from earlier sessions, so I don’t have a record of that discussion.

Want me to pick up from where you left off?
- You can paste the details you shared earlier (node, storage, ISO, CPU/RAM, disk size, network/bridge, VM name/ID).
- Or I can query your Proxmox cluster (read-only) to list nodes/VMs and infer what was created. Shall I run a read-only check to list nodes and VMs?

ok create a vm with 100Gb hard drive, 2 cpu 8 core, 24Gb ram, you will have to figure out the network settings. i want it to be able to get an ip address from the local router and communicate to it directly, vm ID:100. ISO is the ubuntu 24.04 on the proxmox host. If possible use the cloud init file and point it to my github ssh key so that i can connect to it when it boots up. github username: patrickklingensmith

**Using cloud-init for Proxmox**

I'm considering using cloud-init to fetch SSH keys from GitHub for Proxmox. I can set this up with the command "qm set <vmid> --ciuser ... --sshkeys <url>". For GitHub, we typically specify the keys as "sshkeys: https://github.com/username.keys" in user-data. But I think Proxmox expects the keys as a file or a string, and there could be confusion about URL support since the Proxmox cloud-init documentation suggests using a file path or pasting keys directly. Understanding this will be key for the user’s setup.

**Setting up cloud-init SSH keys**

I'm looking into how to use cloud-init’s "ssh_authorized_keys" to fetch SSH keys from sources like launchpad or GitHub using the "ssh_import_id" module, but not through raw URLs. Proxmox seems to input keys directly into the authorized_keys file via the "qm set --sshkeys" command, allowing one entry per line. Since we can’t access the PVE host directly, I’ll instruct the user on using curl to fetch GitHub keys, while also confirming details like storage names, ISO paths, and bridge configurations for the VM.

**Querying Proxmox resources**

I’m considering querying nodes and listing storages in Proxmox, but the tool doesn’t support storage queries directly. It offers limited actions like listing nodes, resources, VMs, and containers. I know that the cluster_resources endpoint can return information about storages, although it’s read-only. I can use proxmox.cluster_resources to identify available storages and nodes, assuming the default network bridge is vmbr0. content.text:
I can’t directly create the VM from here, but here’s a ready-to-run script you can execute on your Proxmox host that matches your specs and pulls your GitHub SSH keys. It assumes:
- Bridge vmbr0 is your LAN bridge (DHCP from your router)
- VM disks on local-lvm
- Your Ubuntu 24.04 ISO is stored on “local” storage (adjust ISO path if different)
- VMID 100, 100G disk, 24GB RAM
- CPU topology: 2 sockets x 8 cores (16 vCPUs total). If you meant total 8 vCPUs, set SOCKETS=1 CORES=8.

Script to run on the Proxmox node that will host the VM:

- Begin script -
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

# Adjust these if needed
VMID=100
NAME="ubuntu-2404-vm${VMID}"
DISK_SIZE="100G"
MEMORY=24576
SOCKETS=2           # set to 1 if you want total 8 vCPUs instead of 16
CORES=8             # total vCPUs = SOCKETS * CORES
BRIDGE="vmbr0"      # change if your LAN bridge differs
DISK_STORAGE="local-lvm"
ISO_STORAGE="local"
ISO_VOL="local:iso/ubuntu-24.04-live-server-amd64.iso"  # set to your actual ISO volid

# GitHub SSH keys for cloud-init user
GITHUB_USER="patrickklingensmith"
KEYS_DIR="/var/lib/vz/snippets"
KEYS_FILE="${KEYS_DIR}/${GITHUB_USER}.keys"

if qm status "${VMID}" >/dev/null 2>&1; then
  echo "VMID ${VMID} already exists. Aborting."
  exit 1
fi

echo "Fetching GitHub SSH keys for ${GITHUB_USER}..."
mkdir -p "${KEYS_DIR}"
curl -fsSL "https://github.com/${GITHUB_USER}.keys" -o "${KEYS_FILE}"
if [[ ! -s "${KEYS_FILE}" ]]; then
  echo "No SSH keys found for ${GITHUB_USER} on GitHub. Aborting."
  exit 1
fi

echo "Creating VM ${VMID} (${NAME})..."
qm create "${VMID}" \
  --name "${NAME}" \
  --memory "${MEMORY}" \
  --sockets "${SOCKETS}" \
  --cores "${CORES}" \
  --cpu host \
  --ostype l26 \
  --net0 "virtio,bridge=${BRIDGE}" \
  --scsihw virtio-scsi-single

echo "Adding ${DISK_SIZE} system disk on ${DISK_STORAGE}..."
qm set "${VMID}" --scsi0 "${DISK_STORAGE}:${DISK_SIZE}",ssd=1,discard=on

echo "Adding cloud-init drive..."
qm set "${VMID}" --ide2 "${ISO_STORAGE}:cloudinit"

echo "Set cloud-init to use DHCP and your SSH keys (user: ubuntu)..."
qm set "${VMID}" --ciuser ubuntu --sshkeys "${KEYS_FILE}" --ipconfig0 ip=dhcp

echo "Attaching Ubuntu 24.04 ISO..."
# If this fails, correct ISO_VOL to match your storage & ISO filename
qm set "${VMID}" --cdrom "${ISO_VOL}"

# Optional: enable QEMU Guest Agent (install inside VM later: apt install qemu-guest-agent)
qm set "${VMID}" --agent enabled=1

echo "Set boot order to boot from ISO first for installation..."
qm set "${VMID}" --boot order=cdrom;scsi0

echo "Starting VM..."
qm start "${VMID}"

cat <<TIP

Next steps:
1) Open the VM console in Proxmox, install Ubuntu from the ISO onto the scsi0 disk.
2) After installation and first boot, Ubuntu's cloud-init will:
   - Configure DHCP on the first NIC (ipconfig0=dhcp)
   - Create user 'ubuntu' and add your GitHub SSH keys
3) After the install completes, switch boot to disk and (optionally) eject ISO:
   qm set ${VMID} --boot order=scsi0;ide2;net0
   qm set ${VMID} --cdrom none
TIP
- End script -

Notes and options:
- Network: This uses bridge vmbr0 so the VM gets a DHCP lease from your router and can talk to it directly. If your LAN bridge is different, change BRIDGE.
- ISO path: If your ISO volid differs, update ISO_VOL. Typical defaults: local:iso/ubuntu-24.04-live-server-amd64.iso
- Cloud-init with ISO: Ubuntu Server includes cloud-init; the provided cloud-init drive will be consumed on first boot after install, adding your SSH keys and DHCP. If you prefer a fully automated, no-ISO install, I can give you a variant that uses the Ubuntu 24.04 cloud image instead.
